speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 1, 1995. Our survivor is Albert Feldman. Our interviewer is Rona Arato. We are in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. And the language of the interview shall be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Albert Feldman. Today is June 1st, 1995. We're in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. I'm Rona Arato. And the interview will be conducted in English. Would you please state your name, the date of your birth, and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you speak a little louder, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Please state your name, the date of your birth, and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Albert Feldman, born in the Czechoslovakia, in Breznica. Really, I was born in Hungary on January 5, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you describe your life in Breznia before the war? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breznica--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breznica--

speaker: interviewee
text: --before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was born, it was Hungary. And then four years later, in 1918, it became Czechoslovakia. Well, I stayed in Breznica in Slovakia until I graduated from high school, which was in Bratislava. And after graduation from high school, I went to Prague to university. And in 1938, I received a degree-- an engineering degree and started to teach commercial high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in your home, in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us, who did you live with as a child-- your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my mom, my dad, and my grandmother, my father's mother, and my father's sister Kathy who was separated or divorced from her husband. So I had three-- four-- three ladies around me and one man. That was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Herman. My mother's name was Selma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Eisler. And my aunt was Kathy. And my-- I don't remember my grandmother's name, first name. And I don't remember my grandfather. He was dead by the time I was born. My mom had two children before me. They died in-- very shortly after their birth. So I was the third one. And they were very anxious to keep me alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: My religious background-- it was two Jewish family in a Slovakian village of-- of Breznica, and about 1,500 inhabitants, mainly farmers. And the-- the other Jewish family was my father's sister, Aunt Hanna. And they had a farm. And we had a store.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my religious background-- whatever I learned from-- about Judaism was my father, who was religious, and my mom. And we kept a-- all the holidays, Jewish holidays, especially the Seder, Pesach. And my father was conducting the Seder and telling his stories from the war, from the First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was listening, but every year the stories were the same, so it got boring. But I still remember all of them. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the relations like with the non-Jewish people in the town? Was there any anti-Semitism, or how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any anti-Semitism. They liked my parents. The people liked my parents. And we had a store. And my-- they had tried to help out people as much as he could. So this-- whatever you're trying to get me to all happened after '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish institutions in the area-- synagogues in neighboring towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the neighboring town-- the closest town was [INAUDIBLE]. It was six kilometers away. And between us in Breznica and [INAUDIBLE] there was a river called [? Ohre. ?] And we could-- to get to the train, we have to be transferred to the other side by a ferry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any time the water was too high, we were isolated or the ice-- the river was frozen, we had to wait until the ice is solid enough to take us over. So we were really isolated. And there was a sort of a synagogue, a small place with about 10, 15 Jewish families in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And to get to the-- to the small town, we very seldom went to the holidays. Because we did everything, all the religious services, at home. My father was conducting it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started school in Breznica-- first five grades. Then my parents wanted to give me some Jewish education. So I was taken to live with my aunt in [INAUDIBLE], which was about 40, 50 kilometers away from Breznica. It was a small town, having maybe 15,000 inhabitants. And I lived with my aunt-- with my aunt and her daughter who was married. And I attended Jewish school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I was about 10, 11, or 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there a short time. And I came back to Breznica. And my parents arranged my bar mitzvah in [INAUDIBLE] in the little synagogue. And then I went to school to Levice, Leva, which became-- it was Czechoslovakia, what was a Hungarian time-- time-- town of 15,000 people. And there was Jewish schools. And there were about 3,000 Jewish people living there out of 15,000 inhabitants that Leva had, Levice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you-- could you spell the name of that town for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Czech name or Slovakian name is Levice, L-E-V-I-C-E, The Hungarian name-- it was a Hungarian town, really. The majority spoke Hungarian. And the spelling is L-E apostrophe V-A, pronounced Leva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I finished medium high school there-- medium high school, right. And then I went to Bratislava to finish my high school and graduated with high school graduation degree. And that was a Hungarian high school-- so three years, four years in Levice and four years in Bratislava to finish my high school degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were in high school or before, how often did you get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from Levice, I just about every weekend to Breznica. And then from Bratislava, I was just for-- for-- for the holidays-- for holidays and for the semester or the summer holidays when school ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could to describe a Shabbat in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat?

speaker: interviewer
text: A Shabbat-- how did your family celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we closed the store Friday night. And we had the candles on. And Mom baked a challah. And it was our Friday night service. So we said some prayers. That was it for Friday night. And Saturday, the store was operated by a helper. And my parents stood away from the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on holidays, you celebrated at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We celebrated the holidays at home, yeah. My father was a heavy cigar smoker. But he couldn't smoke on Saturday. So he was very anxious to have Saturday over, the holiday-- Sabbath over. And he started to sing. So that was his past time. And somehow, I may have inherited his lovely voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he sing for the holidays? When did he sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- he was quite a natural singer. And to fill his time waiting for end of Sabbath so he can smoke again, he filled it with singing. And thus I learned it from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of songs? What did he sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was prayer songs-- Hebrew prayer songs, and some Hungarian songs, and some Yiddish songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, tell-- what was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she had no voice. Yeah, she had no singing voice. No. She was a good cook, she was, and baker. And-- and my mother was very strict with me, while the Aunt Kathy was very giving.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anytime my mother chased me out of the house and no food, so I-- I hid in the alleyway behind the house. And Aunt Kathy brought some food so my mother couldn't see it and see if she fed me so I shouldn't go hungry. That was Aunt Kathy. Describe your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the house that you lived in. What was the house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Describe the house? Well, we didn't have indoor-- what's the word?

speaker: interviewer
text: Plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Indoor plumbing. So we had to walk through a-- a-- a way between the house to take us to the out-- out-- outdoor house. And that's what we did. And then there was the store. And there was a big room that they used-- the young men came to have a drink in the evening before they went to see their girlfriends. So it was like an inn. And the store was open doing the day. In the evening, it was closed. But the inn was operating. And then there was two bedrooms and a big kitchen. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went-- finished high school in Bratislava, what-- where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bratislava-- I'm not realizing Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: After high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went to synagogues. I was in a choir, a religious choir. I don't remember too much until I graduated somehow. Bratislava was a big town, big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a capital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- there were about 200,000 maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It sure was very large, the Jewish community. It was German speaking, Slovakian speaking, Hungarian speaking, one third of each. And the Jews were part of each section. And there was really, in Bratislava, was a ghetto. And the Jewish people-- some Jewish people lived there from the olden days. And it was-- yeah, a man was going around before Sabbath came in every Friday night, and announcing every high-- high rise. In all the homes where Jewish people lived, they announced that Sabbath is on. So this was so stuck to my mind. It was sort of before it was a ghetto where the Jewish people were closed off. But it was open. But still, everybody knew that this is the old ghetto. And some-- a lot of Jewish people lived there still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any Jewish organizations while you lived there-- youth groups or anything of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bratislava-- yes, I was in swim club-- Bar Kochba, Bar Kochba, yes. I-- I became a good swimmer, which stayed with me all my life. And I started my swimming career, really, in Levice-- career. I shouldn't call it a career. I was-- I was just an amateur. But I spent-- I played water polo in Levice. And I was sort of a star there. I was the best swimmer there. In Bratislava, then I became-- so I had instructions for swimming. And I played water polo there. And that's-- I was very much interested in-- in light athletics-- that's running, and-- and discus throwing, and shot put. And Hungarian-- from Budapest, Hungarian athletes came over to play against Bratislava's athletes. And I was always throwing back the-- the shot put to the guy-- the big guys. I-- I was really at their feet, you know, just adoring them. But I-- I was nothing-- I couldn't do anything really good except swimming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were there any Jewish organizations that were active at the time, youth organizations that you either became involved in or were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the swim club, I said, they named it Bar Kochba, Bar Kochba, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends in Bratislava Jewish, or non-Jewish, or mix? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- this was a mixed high school. So it was some Jewish, some-- some-- you mix Christians. And my-- my best friend was Jewish. The other best friend was Christian. He married a Jewish girl. And really, he-- he was-- this Christian fellow became an officer in the army, he was instrumental when the war was over-- we went-- I lived in Prague. And we went to see-- back to Levice, see who was left over. And when we came back, there was my wife, or my fiancee then, I guess. And she was with me. And we came back to Bratislava. It was night. And the Russian soldiers were pretty dangerous with young woman-- women around. So we waited for a train to go back to Prague. And it was night. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- that was after the war, right? That was after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, were-- do you recall any anti-Semitism or anti-Semitic incidents--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were living in Prague or in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no such thing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt very integrated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We went to synagogues. And the other people went to their churches. But it was-- the school was integrated, that was-- we didn't feel any-- any such thing as anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue any Jewish studies while you were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bratislava, did you continue in a Jewish studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became-- I-- I think I became a member of church-- a synagogue, pardon, not a church-- a synagogue choir. And it was a big synagogue. And the cantor was a famous man by the name [? Stern, ?] who I think went to London, England, where he performed his trade as a cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you graduated high school, after high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After high school, I came back to Levice. And my-- my dad [? lent ?] me a job as a-- as a helper in an office. And shortly after, I-- I was courting a young girl. And I guess the parents-- her parents were very quite rich. And I wasn't enough for-- for-- for the girl-- sort of didn't have any social standing as a just graduate from high school and working in an office. So I decided, just for the sake of the girl, to start university. But a year later, she married somebody else, and the whole thing fell apart. But I continued in my studies at the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was studying-- studying accountancy commercial and leading me towards an engineering degree in commerce. That's how I came to Canada with the engineer of commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was teaching. Beg your pardon-- I was teaching in Prague. When I graduated, I was teaching high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you graduate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated in 1938. And then the Germans marched in March 15, 1939. And I had to leave school-- this high school-- because the Jewish teachers and Jewish children or students were not allowed to continue their studies. And in this situation in Prague was there was 80,000 Jews lived in Prague. And the lawyers were not allowed to continue their practice. And the Jewish kids had no education, were excluded from studies-- studying in public schools. And the Jewish Congress-- the Jewish-- they would call it-- well, the offices of the Jewish Congress, I guess, they would call it here-- they needed Jewish teachers to teach these students privately in homes, 10 kids in one home. So they asked me, since I was a teacher. Not too many Jewish teachers were around. They asked me to lead one of these groups. And they assigned three lawyers, Jewish lawyers, who couldn't practice to me as assistants. And we had every day school in a Jewish home-- 10 kids, one lawyer with one kid-- with a group of 10 kids. I was with the others. The other lawyer was with another group of 10 kids in the home. This went on until the-- the transportation or deportation of Jews started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this started when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In started in, I guess '41. '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the teaching started-- when-- when did you start teaching the Jewish children? That was is 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as the Germans marched and they disbanded the-- the professions-- Jewish lawyers, I told you. And teachers and students couldn't continue their studies in schools. So the Congress was interested so these kids should be following their education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to Germans coming in, do you-- what was the climate like in Prague? Was there anti-Semitism at that point? Were you beginning to feel that things were changing? What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the Germans actually came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Czechs were against the German-- German occupation. They were very free thinking, and the religion was no problem. They liked us just as much, because we were integrated to the Czech life with the Czechs. We had Czech friends. And it was-- they were with us against the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So. OK, in 1939 the Germans came in, and you left the school. What other changes were there in your lifestyle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my lifestyle-- well, we were assigned-- all the Jewish people-- Prague had about 15 districts. And the Jews were allowed to live only in district one. That was Old Prague. So we-- myself and a colleague of mine who was an architectural student were assigned to a Jewish lady who was a widow. She had a five room apartment. And we shared one room with this old lady. And that's what the Germans did, that the Jews should be concentrated in Old Prague. So this is about it. And shortly after that, in '42, I was deported to Theresienstadt. But before that, I had a cousin who was from Levice. And she lived in Prague. She was married there, had a child there. And every Saturday, she would ask me to come for dinner-- Saturday noon. And one day, I came for dinner Saturday. And she told me, I have to tell you a story. So I sat down. She said, my husband, Alex, walking in Prague met somebody who he know from before. And this man said, I am an officer in the Gestapo. And what are you doing here, Jew, he said. They knew each other from before. And this guy said, I'm a major in the Gestapo. But I'd like to help you. And if you have any relatives or friends, I help them along. So she-- my cousin, Martha, said, how lucky we are. He's going to help us to get out of Prague. And she said, well, since you're related to me, of course it will cost us 60,000 crowns. But I have not the money. But we'll forward the money. And when we get back, we'll come back to Leva, your parents will reimburse us for the money. And she spoke-- she said the major will come and talk to us. And after, he said that he wants to meet with me. So I came on Friday evening to my cousin, to Martha. And the major was sitting in the den. And in front of him was a bottle of wine. And he was red in his face. He was a drinker. And I was sitting across from him. And he told me that since he is friendly with Alex and Martha, he wants to help me to get over to Leva where there are no-- no problems with Jewish people. Because Hungary isn't occupied.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia became Hungary in '38, or part of my Breznica and my Leva became Hungary. And I sort of-- I asked him a very funny question. I said, major, why would you do this as a German to help Jews to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a teacher. And anything that was on my mind was on my mouth. I said, whatever, I have to risk it. But I'm going to ask him this question. And he got the right in his face. And he, almost in a screaming way, he told me, well, once, everybody has to die. He said it in German-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I said, something went through my mind. You said die. But gee, I don't want to die yet. Because, I said, if they catch you in the train as you carry-- go with us in the train out of-- out of Prague, and the MP, the military police, would come in, and the whole thing blew out, your life would be in danger. So would our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't worry about it. I'm [? not-- ?] you have to die anyway once. So I said, maybe you free to die. But I want to live. And this went through my mind. I said, should I do it? Shouldn't I do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a girlfriend who was mixed-- from a mixed marriage. The father was Jewish. The mother was Christian. And I spent one night there. Because the major said, at 12 o'clock Saturday, I meet you at a train station under the big clock with three other people who will go along.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just take very few things with you, because in 24 hours we will be in your home town, in Leva. And I spent the night there with my girlfriend, Vera, and her mother. And we discussed this. Should I go with him, or what's the outcome of this? I'm not decided. I can't make up my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a-- you know, I really affected them with my problem-- my-- my girlfriend's mother. And finally, towards the morning-- we couldn't sleep-- towards the morning, mother-- my girlfriend's mother said, look. You're a healthy guy. You're strong. They only take you-- the Germans only take you for work if they deport you. You'll survive this. So my advice to you is forget about the major. Forget about going with him. Go and be deported. And it will be only a working assignment. That made up my mind. It-- OK, saved my life-- wow, saved my life. Shortly after, maybe six weeks later, I was deported with transport AN558 to Theresienstadt. And about a few weeks later, sitting in the barracks I was-- and eating my meal there, two people-- one was an old-timer in Theresienstadt, only a few-- few months already. And the other one was a newcomer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these two guys were talking to each other. And one, the new-- the newcomer said, did you hear what happened in Prague? No, he said. We have no news. We can't listen to radio. We know nothing about outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, a Jewish fellow got friendly-- was friendly with the major from the Gestapo. And this Gestapo major arranged Jews to get out of the country. He will lead them out of the country. And he had some people in the train. And the first station where the train stopped, he delivered-- called in the MPs. And all these people were-- died. Because they were as Jews fleeing the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just got to pause.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, gee, it's my story. He said, I'm alive. I didn't go with him. I'd be dead. Your turn--

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to pause for a moment.
